speaker: interviewer
text: OK, yep.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 2 of the Mr. Thomas Simon interview. You were telling me about the implementation of the ghetto in Pecs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: We moved into-- I don't remember the exact date, but I think sometime in May, we were forced to move into the ghetto. As I had mentioned, we had one room for the four of us, which was quite a change from the previous location, where, of course, we had a whole house. But the circumstances weren't all that severe, even in the ghetto. I, I think we had enough food to eat. It wasn't anything like the Warsaw Ghetto, for instance, that-- where things were a lot more, more terrible. And we, we were actually-- as kids, actually, we were-- we didn't go to school, of course, but there was-- we were organized into some organized activities and-- including private tutoring type of thing. And we had some, some other-- but they-- it, it was made sure that we'll have organized activities for the children for the period that we were there, which was about six weeks. At the end of that period-- and I'm not sure, again, of the exact dates-- we were taken away from the ghetto to a, a-- some kind of a-- an, an-- a gendarmerie camp. Basically, it was like-- it was stables for horses, I think, that they kept the gendarmerie in. And we were-- we had to stay in that area. Also, it was in Pecs. And that was a lot-- the conditions were a lot more severe in those-- in, in, in that camp, or gendarmerie camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you entered the gendarmerie camp, how was food given to you? How did you survive in that way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't honestly recall how my mother got food. I mean, we had some money. We were allowed to, I believe, take some money with us. And we could buy, presumably, food. And so I, I presume there was still some kind of commerce between the ghetto and the outside world. But I don't know the details. I just know that we weren't starving. And conditions weren't really that terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a large Nazi presence felt, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: In, in Pecs, in general?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Pecs, in the ghetto, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think so. I, I think the ghetto itself was under the jurisdiction of the regular Hungarian police or city police or-- and there's a-- we should really differentiate between the regular police and the gendarmerie. And the regular police were a lot more humane in general. Again, there might be exceptions. But in general, they were a lot more humane than the gendarmerie, who were quite cruel-- the latter. And when the-- when we-- when we lost-- when they-- when the police lost control of us to the gendarmerie, things got a lot more hairy. And then things got really very, very bad. The conditions were poor. And that was in that gendarmerie camp. We only stayed there for a week. That was just prior to the actual deportation.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was it from the ghetto to the gendarmerie camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I, I-- could have been a couple of miles. I, I-- it would-- I, I know we walked over there. And then I-- I'm pretty sure we weren't transported. We were walked over there. And it wasn't very far. I mean, Pecs is not a large city. And so it couldn't have been more than, I would say, a couple of miles at most.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you bring all the things you brought into the ghetto to the gendarmerie camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We were only allowed to take very little, I mean, whatever we could carry to, to that gendarmerie station. And so we had very little to, to-- of our belongings there. And I know we, we, we actually slept-- stayed and slept in, in, in stalls in there that were for, for horses, actual stables. I don't know what they did with the horses. But, [LAUGHS] but that's where we were quartered, in, in those-- in, in, in those stables for about a week. And we didn't have-- I-- you know, I recall, for instance, as opposed to what happened in the ghetto, where we had sanitary facilities still-- in the-- in, in that gendarmerie station camp, we only had latrines. And first-- my first experience with-- you know, as an 11-year-old boy-- with a latrine, wasn't a very pleasant one. And it was a very different situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were at the gendarmerie camp? Would you hazard a guess?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I think all the ghetto. In fact, what happened was that not just the Jews of Pecs, who were vacated-- evacuated from the ghetto ended up there, but also Jews that were rounded up from surrounding areas. And that's how, actually, our family got united. We got-- our-- my extended family on my mother's side all ended up in that same area so that we were actually together, some-- over a dozen, probably about 18 people. And, and we all-- that's where we met. And, and we stuck together. And in fact, we were deported together in the same cattle car.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in some sense, at-- you know, at that time, my mother was probably quite happy to see her mother and her sisters and, and other relatives at the same place. But that was just, you know, fortuitous, in a way, that we all ended up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so about a week-- I think it was six days or seven days that we were in that-- under the control of the gendarmerie. And then we were taken to-- back to-- sort of backtracking the town to the-- to the railway station, again walking. And we were put in the cattle cars and deported. And, of course, we had no idea, absolutely no idea where are we going, how long the journey is going to take. We had absolutely no idea of, of where we going to end up. We were just put in those cattle cars. And, and, and the train started. I think the date was July 6, 1944, when we [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] boarded those trains-- that train.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were on your-- were on your car?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess about 75. I, I, I don't know the exact number. But it was pretty crowded.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you on the train for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three days. We arrived in Auschwitz, actually in Birkenau, on the 9th of July. So we have-- we were three days. It was pretty horrific. I don't have too much of my recollection of, of those three days. But it was pretty horrific. I mean, sanitary conditions were nonexistent. And, and, and, and there was very little water and food. And, and so it's-- and, you know, all those people on the same train.

speaker: interviewer
text: It must have been very hot, as it was in the summer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Obviously, it was very, very-- and there were only two little windows. I mean, they had sort of openings in the cattle car, I think, you know, little windows. They weren't windows. They were just openings. So there was very, very little air. And, and it was gruesome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of the 75 or so people on your car, do you remember how many people got off the train alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't honestly remember anybody actually dying on the plane-- on the train. I, I-- there may have been fatalities. I don't know. I, I can't recall. I know people getting sick. I don't know if anybody actually died on that train whilst, you know, in, in the three days. There may, may have been some. But I, I am not aware.

speaker: interviewer
text: So up to this point, your 18 or so members of family are still all together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there were 18 of us together on-- 18 of my extended family on that-- in the same cattle car. And so on the 9th of July, we arrived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I think it was the afternoon when we were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] de-trained and sort of lined up on a platform of some, some kind of a ramp at Birkenau. And again, we still didn't have any idea what kind of a place it was. I mean, we had-- we never heard-- I mean, I recall seeing, or my mother seeing actually a sign saying 'Auschwitz.' But, but, I mean, it didn't mean a thing. And at that point, we were lining up. And we were-- we saw, of course, those strange-looking people with, with prisoners' garb, you know, with the striped garb. Again, we didn't know what to make of it. And, and there was quite a bit of commotion. And the SS, of course, were not very helpful. And as we were lining up there, an SS officer came along, he sort of went along the line there, the column, and, and called out 'Zwillinge, Zwillinge.' And that means 'twins' in German. I didn't know, either, [LAUGHS] at the time. My mother, of course, spoke German. And she knew what it meant. So she put up her hand or whatever and pointed to us, that we are twins. And that-- so the German officer said for us to get out-- get out of the line. And, of course, I didn't know at that time that, that that officer was Mengele. And, and my mother was hesitant. But I think she had the feeling that it's for the better. Previous to actually arriving in Auschwitz, I recall vividly that she told us, both my brother and I, that if anything happens and we're separated, we should claim that we're not 11 years old, but only nine years old. Because she had that idea if you're less than 10 years old, you'll be treated better. Now-- [LAUGHS] so we actually, throughout our stay in Auschwitz, we were known as nine-year-olds. We were-- because we didn't, of course, dare to later on reveal that it's a lie. So when we were told to tell our birth date, we changed from 1933 to 1935. And we stayed. And all our friends, all our-- all the other twins, of course, in, in-- they didn't know that. We were-- we were considered pretty smart for nine-year-olds, [LAUGHS] which was kind of an advantage in some ways. But that was because my mother told us not to say we're 11, in case we're asked. But to get back to that point, we, we were at that point separated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see your mother again?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I never saw my mother and sister again. I never heard of them. And I never saw them again. And, and I must assume-- and it's strictly an assumption on my part, that they perished on that same day.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when it comes to their yahrzeit, we [CRYING] actually commemorate that day, which is July 9th, 1944. [CRYING] It's, it's the-- in, in Hebrew date, it's the 18th of Tammuz. So we use the 18th of Tammuz. But we have absolutely no idea whether, in fact, that was the date. So we-- that's-- it's just-- [CRYING] you have to pick a date.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's when we were taken over-- [CRYING] we were taken over to another-- some barracks. And, and, and actually, we were taken by ambulance, I think, to-- there was an ambulance nearby, an ambulance car. And we were taken by ambulance to a-- to a-- an area which turned out to be the, the camp for the-- for the twins.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were tattooed at that point, both my brother and I. And that's where we met all the other twins that had already been gathered previously.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you show us your tattoo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I can. Let me just unroll. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It's A17455.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take for them to actually tattoo you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too long. What they do, they, they don't do it like branding a, a cattle. But they use something like-- looks like a pin. As I recall, it was like pinpricks. And it's-- really, it looks continuous. But in fact, it's, it's, it's little dots that sort of merge. And it was not all that painful. But, but I, I somehow realized that-- I somehow realized that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's permanent, even though I was only 11. And, I-- you know, I never had any experience of that nature before. I, I realized that, that it's going to be permanent.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened right after they tattooed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we just stayed there. And I, I recall we did a lot of crying because we had no idea was going to happen with us. We had no idea what happened to my mother and sister. And, and we were somewhat comforted by the other inmates.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, that's an-- that's a question I've been asking myself a lot. Because I don't recall exactly. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think there were less than a hundred pairs of twins in, in where we were. There may have been 50 or 75. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Since then, I've heard other numbers from various people. For one thing, the girls, the twin girls, were, were in a different area. They were not with us. It was strictly men, boys-- males. And females were in a different area.

speaker: interviewer
text: So even when, when a-- when a twin happened to be a boy and a girl, they were separated. And so we only had the, the males with us. And most of them were children. There were a few adults.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we also had in the same camp, in the same barracks, some dwarfs. Because Mengele was not only interested in twins, he was also interested in dwarves, midget dwarves. And and we had quite a few of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was kind of a-- I've never seen that many dwarves in one place before. And I've never seen, of course, that many twins in one place. Some were identical. Some were not.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were mostly from Hungary because I believe Mengele didn't actually start these so-called experiments until about 1943. So it, it was-- most of his material came from Hungary. There were a few Czechs, I believe. But most of them from Hungarian population, Jewish population. And so when we arrived there, we were one of the last to arrive in that. There was only one-- as I recall, there was only one other pair of twins that arrived about two days after us. They're-- they were from a suburb of Budapest. Because Budapest itself was not-- the Jews of Budapest largely survived. They were not deported. But some of the suburbs were. And these two-- and I remember them. I mean, I, I, I, I know them very well because I've met them after the war many times. In fact, one of them, one of that couple-- their, their, their, their name was Brichta, B-R-I-C-H-T-A. And one of the two happens to be today the Ambassador of Israel to Budapest. They live-- both live in Israel. And the other one-- the other, his, his brother, he works for an insurance company in Jerusalem.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their first names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Robert and Karoly. Of course, they've changed their name, too. They're now called Alon. And they're, they're called Mordechai and-- what was the other? I can't remember. But they're, they're of course, their Hebrew names. And, and he was-- I met Mordechai Alon actually not too long-- I, I met him in the Israeli embassy in The Hague some years ago before he became an ambassador. And then he became an ambassador to Norway first. During the Oslo talks, he was the ambassador to-- Israeli ambassador to-- in Oslo. So he-- it's obvious that he's doing very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, those two were the-- those-- the only two who, who arrived after us. So we were at the tail end of that-- of the group.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you at that point? Where did you sleep? And what were your living conditions like?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. The living conditions were pretty bad. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we slept on, on wooden-- what would you call them? Bunks-- several levels. And it was pretty crowded.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people to a bunk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, god, I don't know. I don't remember. I mean, it depends on the size of the bunk. I, I don't know. We were-- it was crowded. I know it was crowded. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were-- when we arrived, of course, it was summer. So we, we didn't, certainly, suffer from frost or, or, or frostbites or cold. But later on, during the winter, things were very much worse as far as-- there was no sufficient heating. And, and we were always, constantly cold. The food we got was very inadequate, of course. It was some kind of soup and a little bit of bread with sawdust in it. Now kids-- and that's interesting-- kids that were less than 10 years old got some milk. And so I, I think my mother must have been-- I mean, it was really something for her to remember-- to, to, to, to, to recognize it and realize that that might help. And as, as nine-year-olds, so to speak, we did get some milk every night, I think-- not much. But-- we were hungry. We were very hungry. But we weren't starving. We, we did lose a lot of weight. But that wasn't-- we were still better off, I think, than most people in Auschwitz. Now, as far as the-- what we were doing, what was done to us, again, my brother and I, I think, were lucky, maybe because we were at the tail end of this-- the group. We weren't experimented on to the same extent and some-- as some of the others. I recall-- obviously I don't recall everything that was done. The, the, the routine used to be that what-- Mengele would pick a pair of twins and have them transported to his office or his, his, his lab or whatever. And he would do experiments on that pair. And then they would go back. And then-- and then the next day or next week or whatever, another pair. I mean, I, I-- his experiments-- I, I mean, I-- [SIGHS] in retrospect, they were really phony. I mean, I, I, I understand. I'm not certainly not a medical doctor, or anything like that. But, but as I understand it, they were-- they were-- they didn't have much scientific value. That's my understanding. But, of course, at that time, I had no idea. Most of the time, when we were called and taken to his office, we were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they took blood from us. A number of-- I must have had six or seven times they took blood from me and my brother. I can't remember how much. I have, of course, no idea why-- what he did with the blood. Beyond that, he would measure everything on-- I mean, they had the-- he, he, he was-- he was really-- I understand he was an anthropologist by, by, by--

speaker: interviewee
text: Training.

speaker: interviewee
text: --training, if you like. And he did all sorts of measurements on anything from, you know, the head and the eyes. And, and I know one of the most unpleasant things that I can recall was when he took impressions of our teeth. Because I recall that we had to-- they probably used plaster of Paris or some gypsum, some kind of material like calcium sulfate. And, and of course, they filled our teeth, our, our mouth. And it was extremely unpleasant. I mean, I had to have my mouth-- I was gagging and-- for several hours until that thing dried. And they took it out. That was-- that, that imprint, that stayed with me. And, and I'm, I'm still kind of-- I still have a phobia about gagging, probably from that time on. But that was one of the more unpleasant things that they did. As I say, they measured everything and-- from head to toe. And, and he didn't necessarily do everything himself. I mean, he had assistants. Sometimes he did it. Sometimes he would just watch it, and the assistants would do it. So it wasn't necessarily him. But he, he always supervised it. I mean, he was obviously in charge.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he ever talk to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He may have. I mean, I don't speak-- I didn't speak German. I, I spoke a little German. I suppose I understood-- no, not much. I understand he-- as I recall, he spoke a few words Hungarian. I mean, you know, he, he, he could be-- he was a very, very interesting character. I mean, you know, in a perverse kind of way, I owe my life to him. I mean, this is-- this is-- it's really perversion to say that. But, but it's true. I mean, if he hadn't done those experiments, I certainly wouldn't be alive today. Because all the other kids that came to Auschwitz from Pecs in the same transport, none of them survived. I mean, the only ones that survived were my brother and I. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: So, you know, I, I do owe my life to him. But it's, it's, it's really perverse to say that. I mean, it's, it's obviously not because he wanted us to survive-- because he needed us.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- he was a very-- he was a very vain person. I recall-- I understand that the women felt that he was handsome. Of course, I had no judgment on that. But I do recall one instance when he actually came to our barracks. And I don't know what the reason was. But he came to our barracks. And, and at one point, he said-- he want-- he had us all lined up, all the twins. And he wanted to show how good a memory he has. Because he said, I'm going to now pick up-- pick out all the twins that I had picked up when they arrived in Auschwitz. Because not all the twins were picked up by him. Sometimes he asked or, or sent others to go along the lines and say, well, pick out the twins. But sometimes, he did it himself. And there were maybe a dozen pairs where he did it himself. And he, he was able to pick out the ones. And he was very proud of himself because he could pick out-- including ourselves, my, my brother and I-- these are the ones that I picked out myself. I mean he was very, very vain. And he thought very highly of himself. There's no question about it. He was a monster. I mean-- [CRYING] you know, I keep thinking about him and-- from time to time. And, I mean, he was a mass murderer. There's no question about it. But I don't think he was the greatest mass murderer in, in history. I mean, his boss, Hitler, was responsible for a lot more death than he was. Stalin was probably more responsible for more death. And so was Genghis Khan and Attila. But in some sense, he was unique. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was probably-- he was responsible for more death than anyone else who all his victims. Because obviously Genghis Khan didn't know all his victims. And Attila the Hun didn't know all his victims. But Mengele knew all his victims. And by that I mean, of course, he didn't know them by name. But he looked at them. He looked into their eyes before he selected them. He, he, he, he saw these people. And he selected them, one by one. He knew them. And he decided on their fate. In that sense, he was the greatest mass murderer in history. I can't think of anybody else that killed as many people as he did whom he knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll pause there. And we'll pick up on the next tape.
